segments.inferno-26-g15: Così = 'thus/so' (adverb) placed before the verb for emphasis. foss' is a poetic contraction of fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) used here in a colloquial/exclamatory register; ei = egli ('he') is the nominative subject placed after the verb in inverted, archaic word order.
segments.inferno-26-g16: da che = 'since' (introduces the causal clause). pur = 'indeed/still' adding emphasis. esser dee is an archaic/poetic inversion of modern deve essere ('must be'): the infinitive esser precedes the finite verb dee ('must'), so the clause means 'he must (indeed) be'.
segments.inferno-26-g17: ché = 'for/because' giving the reason. più modifies the verb and means 'more'; mi is the indirect object 'to me'; graverà is the future indicative of gravare ('will weigh down / be burdensome'), so 'it will weigh on me more.'
segments.inferno-26-g18: com' (come) introduces the comparative correlative 'the more ... the more' and here begins the subordinate clause. più = 'more'; m' = mi ('to me' / reflexive indirect); attempo is a present-form verb meaning 'I grow old / I mature' — together 'as I grow older' (literally, 'how more I grow old').
segments.inferno-26-g19: Noi (subject pronoun, explicit for emphasis) + ci (reflexive/reciprocal clitic) + partimmo (passato remoto, 1st person plural of partire). The construction literally means 'We departed ourselves' and partimmo is a finished past tense used narratively.
segments.inferno-26-g20: This is a single phrase with a relative clause. e = 'and'; su per = 'up along' (su = 'up'); le scalee = 'the stairways' (plural). che introduces a relative clause where n’ is the enclitic ne referring back to the steps (of them), avea + fatto is an imperfect auxiliary + past participle used like a pluperfect/literary perfect ('had made'), iborni = 'ivory' (describing the steps), a + infinitive (scender) expresses purpose ('for descending'), and pria = 'earlier/before'.
segments.inferno-26-g21: rimontò is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of rimontare = 'he mounted up again'; ’l is the elided article (il) before duca; duca mio = 'my guide' (Dante calls Virgil his duca/guide); e coordinates the two verbs rimontò and trasse; trasse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of trarre = 'he drew/pulled'; mee is an archaic spelling of the object pronoun 'me' (direct object).
segments.inferno-26-g22: e = 'and'; proseguendo is a gerund ('continuing') giving attendant circumstance; la solinga via = 'the solitary/ lone way' (solinga is an archaic form of solinga/solinga meaning solitary). The gerund clause describes what happens while the main action continues.
segments.inferno-26-g23: tra ... e tra ... = 'among/between ... and among ...', giving the location (tra le schegge e tra ’rocchi de lo scoglio). de lo is an archaic form of del (of the). lo piè = 'the foot' is the grammatical subject of the final clause; sanza la man = 'without the hand' (sanza = senza); non si spedia is a reflexive/impersonal negative construction: spediare here means 'hasten/proceed quickly' so 'the foot, without the hand, does not hasten' (i.e. the foot cannot move quickly without support from the hand).
segments.inferno-26-g24: Allor = 'then' (temporal adverb); mi is the reflexive/dative clitic; dolsi is passato remoto, 1st person singular of dolersi (to be pained/grieve) — 'I grieved/I felt pain.'
segments.inferno-26-g25: e ora mi ridoglio = 'and now I grieve again' (ridoglio is present tense 'I grieve again'); quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when/whenever'; drizzo la mente = present indicative 'I direct/turn the mind'; a ciò = 'to that/to this'; ch’io vidi = 'that I saw' (che + past historic vidi). The sequence sets a habitual/recurring emotion when Dante recalls what he witnessed.
segments.inferno-26-g26: e più lo ’ngegno affreno ch’i’ non soglio = 'and I restrain my intellect more than I am wont.' più ... affreno expresses a comparative 'more ... I restrain'; lo ngegno (archaic spelling of ingegno) = 'the intellect/wit'; affreno is present indicative 'I curb/rein in'; ch’ introduces the comparison clause; i (here stands for io, the subject 'I') and non soglio ('I am not wont') is a fixed expression meaning 'than I usually do' — the overall meaning is 'more than I am accustomed to restrain my wit.'
segments.inferno-26-g27: perché introduces a purposive/result clause here ('so that'); non corra uses the present subjunctive corra ('it run') expressing the idea 'so that it will not run'; che introduces the exception/conditional sense 'unless'; virtù is the guiding principle ('virtue' or guiding power); nol = 'not it' (negation + clitic lo) and guidi is subjunctive 'may/must guide' — overall: 'so that it will not run unless virtue guides it.'
segments.inferno-26-g28: sì che = 'so that/therefore' introducing the concluding result or consequence clause that follows.
segments.inferno-26-g29: Conditional protasis: se = 'if'; stella bona = 'a good star' (figuratively: favorable fate or guiding star) or miglior cosa = 'or some better thing'; m’ ha dato = perfect tense (ha + past participle) with enclitic m' = 'has given me'; ’l ben = 'the good' / 'the blessing' — 'if a good star or better thing has given me this good.'
segments.inferno-26-g30: ch’io = 'that I'; stessi is a subjunctive form used optatively ('that I may be' / 'that I should be' — literary usage), nol = 'not it' (negation + lo), m’ = clitic 'to me', invidi is present subjunctive of invidare ('begrudge/envy') meaning 'may I not begrudge it to myself.' The clause expresses the wish/result that Dante should not be envious or begrudging of that good.
segments.inferno-26-g1: Godi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of godere (old/poetic form), a direct address telling Florence to 'rejoice' or 'take pleasure'. It is vocative and stands alone as an exclamation.
segments.inferno-26-g2: Fiorenza is the vocative/name being addressed (medieval form of Firenze). It simply names the city to which the imperative is directed.
segments.inferno-26-g3: Poi che here introduces a causal or concessive clause ('now that' / 'since'); se' is the contracted archaic form of sei (2nd-person sing. of essere) meaning 'you are'; sì grande = 'so great'. The second che introduces a result clause 'that…', and per mare e per terra batti l'ali is literal: per + place ('through/by sea and by land') + batti, present indicative 2nd-person singular of battere ('you beat'), with l'ali = le ali (the wings) shortened by elision.
segments.inferno-26-g4: E = 'and'. Per lo 'nferno = 'through the Inferno'; the initial i of inferno is elided (’nferno) for the verse. Tuo nome = 'your name' or 'your reputation'; si spande is the verb spandere with clitic si forming a passive/impersonal sense: 'is spread' / 'spreads itself'.
segments.inferno-26-g5: Tra li ladron = 'among the thieves' with the archaic article li before the plural noun; trovai is the passato remoto (I found) of trovare; cinque cotali tuoi cittadini = 'five such of your citizens' (cotali = 'such [people]'); onde introduces the result/whence ('from which' / 'whereby') and mi ven vergogna is literal 'shame comes to me' (ven is an older/poetic form of viene).
segments.inferno-26-g6: E tu = 'and you'; in grande orranza = 'into/into great honor' (orranza = honor, homage); non ne sali is negative: non = 'not', ne is a partitive/pronominal adverb referring back to those citizens ('from them' / 'on their account'), and sali is the 2nd-person present of salire 'you climb/rise' used figuratively ('you do not attain/gain') — the precise modern reflex of this particular sali is 'you do not rise/benefit' (here the listed senses for sali did not match that meaning, so see senses field).
segments.inferno-26-g7: Ma = 'but'. Se here is the conditional conjunction 'if' (note: the listed sense for se was for 'you are', so it does not fit here). Presso al mattin del ver = 'near the morning of truth' / 'close to the true dawn' (presso = near; mattin = morning; del ver = of truth/verity, a poetical expression). Si sogna is impersonal present ('one dreams' / 'people dream') using clitic si + verb sognare.
segments.inferno-26-g8: Tu sentirai is simple: tu = 'you', sentirai is the future tense (2nd-singular) of sentire meaning 'you will hear' (or 'you will perceive'). The future places the result in upcoming time.
segments.inferno-26-g9: Di qua da picciol tempo = a temporal/locative phrase meaning 'from here after a short time' or 'before long from this place': di qua = 'from here/this side', da picciol tempo = 'after a short time' (picciol = brief).
segments.inferno-26-g10: Di quel che = 'about/of that which' (di = 'of/about'); quel che = 'that which'. Prato here is the place-name (the town Prato) used as the subject of the following verb; the lemma senses given ('meadow') do not capture the toponymic use, so see senses field.
segments.inferno-26-g11: Non ch'altri = 'not that others' / 'not to mention others': non negates, ch' is the contracted che ('that'), and altri = 'others'. The phrase sets Prato apart from other places/people.
segments.inferno-26-g12: T'agogna = t' (ti) is the clitic object 'you' preceding the verb; agogna is 3rd-person singular present of agognare ('long for', 'yearn for'), so '(Prato) longs for you' or 'desires you'.
segments.inferno-26-g13: E = 'and'. Se già fosse is a conditional protasis: se = 'if' (here a conjunction; the provided 'se' sense 'you are' does not apply), già = 'already', and fosse is the imperfect/subjunctive (or past subjunctive) of essere meaning 'were' ('if it were already so').
segments.inferno-26-g14: Non saria per tempo is the apodosis: non = 'not', saria is the conditional form 'would be' (older/poetic form corresponding to sarebbe), and per tempo means 'timely' / 'in time' — together: 'it would not be in time' or 'it would not be timely'.
segments.inferno-26-g70: This is a conditional clause introduced by an archaic contraction S’ (= se, “if”). Ei posson is present indicative 3pl of potere ('they can'), with the subject pronoun ei placed before the verb as in older/poetic Italian. Dentro da quelle faville uses an emphatic double local expression (dentro + da) meaning ‘from/inside those sparks’; parlar is the infinitive governed by posson and placed finally for poetic rhythm.
segments.inferno-26-g71: Diss' is the contracted, archaic past of dire (disse) with the dropped final vowel marked by an apostrophe; the subject pronoun io follows the verb for emphasis (common in narrative/poetic word order).
segments.inferno-26-g72: Maestro is a direct vocative address (to Virgil); no verb here because it is simply the addressee’s name following the speaker's verb of saying.
segments.inferno-26-g73: Assai is an intensifier ('very/much'), while ten is an archaic clitic form meaning roughly 'to you' (i.e. 'I beg of you'); priego e ripriego are two paired verbs/nouns (entreat/entreat again) forming the plea, with the repetition giving urgency. Word order (clitic before verb) is typical of older Tuscan usage.
segments.inferno-26-g74: Che introduces a subordinate clause expressing result/wish ('that' / 'may it be that'). '’l priego' is the article + priego ('the/this entreaty'), and vaglia is an optative/potential present ('may … avail' / 'be worth') used like a jussive: 'may my plea be worth a thousand.' Mille is simply the numeral.
segments.inferno-26-g75: Another che opens a further subordinate clause. Non mi facci is an archaic subjunctive/request form from fare (here 'do/make [me]'), meaning 'do not make me' or 'do not force me to be (left)'. 'De l'attender niego' = 'of the waiting-denial' i.e. 'deprived of waiting' (niego = denial/negation). Fin che = 'until', and la fiamma cornuta qua vegna is a final time-clause: vegn(a)a is an older subjunctive/optative form of venire ('come'), and cornuta is an adjective 'horned' modifying 'fiamma.'
segments.inferno-26-g76: Vedi here is an imperative/interjection meaning 'see/notice' (addressed to the master). Che again introduces a clause: del disio 'by/with desire' (del = of the + disio = desire). Ver' is an abbreviated/poetic form of verso (toward), and mi piego is a reflexive present 'I bend/turn myself' — 'I turn myself toward her with desire.' The inversion (verb before subject) and truncation ver' are typical of poetic diction.
segments.inferno-26-g77: Simple punctuation: period ending the sentence.
segments.inferno-26-g78: Ed is an archaic/poetic form of e, meaning "and," placed before the speech. elli here is the subject pronoun 'he' (archaic form), but the provided lexical senses do not list a pronoun sense, so it functions as a simple nominative subject. a me is a prepositional phrase 'to me' (a + me) marking the addressee.
segments.inferno-26-g79: La tua preghiera is a noun phrase with the article + possessive + noun: 'your request/prayer.' è is the present indicative of essere ('is'). degna di molta loda is an adjectival phrase: degna (worthy) governs the prepositional phrase di molta loda ('of much praise'), where molta modifies loda.
segments.inferno-26-g80: e io però l'accetto joins the previous praise with a coordinate clause: e (and), io (stressed subject 'I'), però here means 'therefore' (hence), and l'accetto is the verb 'accept' with the clitic direct object pronoun l' (lo) attached to the verb: 'I therefore accept it.'
segments.inferno-26-g81: ma fa che la tua lingua si sostegna. ma ('but') introduces the contrasting injunction. fa che is a causative/imperative construction meaning 'make/see to it that' and typically governs a subjunctive clause; sostegna is an archaic present subjunctive form (modern sostenga) of sostenere, here reflexive si sostegna meaning 'hold itself up' → 'hold back' or 'keep silent.' la tua lingua ('your tongue') is the grammatical subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.inferno-26-g82: Lascia parlare a me is an imperative with lascia (let/allow) followed by the infinitive parlare: 'Let speak to me' → idiomatically 'Let me speak.' The indirect object a me ('to me') emphasizes who should be allowed to speak.
segments.inferno-26-g83: ch’i’ ho concetto: ch’ is the clitic form of che ('that'/'for'); i' is an archaic contraction of io ('I'); ho is the present of avere used here as an auxiliary; concetto is a past participle used transitively to mean 'grasped/understood' (ho concetto = 'I have grasped'). ciò che tu vuoi is a relative clause 'what you want' (ciò che = 'that which'), where vuoi is present indicative of volere.
segments.inferno-26-g84: ch’ei sarebbero schivi: ch’ = 'that'; ei is an archaic third-person plural pronoun 'they' (essi); sarebbero is the conditional (3rd pl. of essere) 'they would be'; schivi is an adjective 'shy/reluctant/averse' agreeing in number with ei. The clause gives the reason why the speaker should speak instead.
segments.inferno-26-g85: perch’ e’ fuor greci: perch’ is shortened perché meaning 'because/for'; e’ here is an enclitic/poetic form related to 'essi' or functioning as the subject pronoun 'they' (in some manuscripts forms vary); fuor is an archaic form of fuori ('outside'/'outside of' or 'are outside/alien'); greci is a predicate noun 'Greeks' (or 'Greek [in language/culture]'). Together the phrase explains the reluctance: 'because they are Greeks (i.e., outsiders).'
segments.inferno-26-g86: forse del tuo detto: forse 'perhaps' hedges the reason; del tuo detto is a prepositional phrase 'of/with regard to your saying/words' (del = di + il, tuo = your, detto = 'saying/words'). The whole short clause qualifies the previous explanation as tentative.
segments.inferno-26-g134: This clause uses a poetic, somewhat compressed word order: two past participles ('racceso' and 'casso') linked by 'e' describe repeated events (’Cinque volte... e tante...’). 'Lo lume era' is a simple imperfect 'was' describing the state of 'the light'. The prepositional phrase 'di sotto da la luna' places that light spatially 'beneath/from the moon' (an idiomatic older phrasing).
segments.inferno-26-g135: 'poi che' is a temporal/concessive conjunction meaning 'after' or 'since'. '’ntrati' is the past participle (archaic contraction of 'intrati'), and 'eravam' is the imperfect used here as an auxiliary giving a pluperfect sense: 'we had entered'. 'ne l’alto passo' is a fused preposition + article + noun meaning 'into the high pass'.
segments.inferno-26-g136: 'quando' = 'when'. The clitic 'n’' (ne) here is an enclitic pronoun meaning 'to us' / 'upon us' (dative/experiential) and is contracted; its exact sense is not among the provided options. 'apparve' is the passato remoto (simple past) 'appeared'. 'una montagna' is a straightforward noun phrase 'a mountain'.
segments.inferno-26-g137: 'bruna' is an adjective placed before the prepositional phrase 'per la distanza', which here means 'because of/by reason of the distance' (explaining why it looked dark). The order 'bruna per la distanza' is typical poetic compression: adjective + causal prepositional phrase.
segments.inferno-26-g138: 'e parvemi' = 'and it seemed to me' where 'parve-mi' is the past tense with enclitic first-person object ('-mi'). 'alta tanto quanto ...' sets up a correlative comparison: 'so high as (tanto ... quanto) ...'. 'veduta non avëa alcuna' is a negative pluperfect construction ('I had seen none'), with the auxiliary 'avëa' (had) and 'alcuna' functioning as 'any/none' after 'non'.
segments.inferno-26-g139: 'Noi ci allegrammo' is reflexive: 'Noi' (we) with reflexive pronoun 'ci' and the verb 'allegrammo'. In older/dialectal/past narrative usage the form indicates a completed action 'we rejoiced/made ourselves glad'. The reflexive pronoun shows the action is performed on the subject.
segments.inferno-26-g140: 'e tosto tornò in pianto' means 'and quickly it turned to weeping' or 'soon returned to weeping'. 'tornò' is third-person singular past (passato remoto) meaning 'turned/returned (to a state)'; 'in pianto' indicates the new state 'into weeping'. The impersonal/third-person subject is the situation or mood rather than 'we'.
segments.inferno-26-g141: 'ché' = 'for/because'. 'de la nova terra' = 'from the new land' (de = of/from). 'un turbo nacque' uses the past historic 'nacque' = 'arose/sprang up'. 'e percosse del legno il primo canto' has 'percosse' (struck) with a direct object 'il primo canto' and 'del legno' specifying whose/which (the wood/ship). The syntax mirrors narrative cause: because X happened, Y followed.
segments.inferno-26-g142: 'Tre volte il fé girar' = 'Three times he caused it to turn': 'Tre volte' (three times) is an adverbial phrase of frequency; 'il' is the object pronoun (the ship); 'fé' is the archaic past form 'fece' (he made/caused); 'girar' is the infinitive 'to turn' used with the causative 'fece'. 'con tutte l’acque' means 'with all the waters' describing the motion.
segments.inferno-26-g143: 'a la quarta' = 'on the fourth (time)'. The clause continues the series of infinitives governed by the prior causative: 'levar la poppa in suso' (to raise the stern up) and 'la prora ire in giù' (to have the prow go/descend down). The parallel infinitives describe the two opposite movements of the ship.
segments.inferno-26-g144: 'com’' is the elided form of 'come' meaning 'as'. 'altrui' = 'others' or 'someone else' (indeterminate genitive/dative sense). 'piacque' is past tense of 'piacere' (it pleased), so the clause means 'as pleased others' or 'as others wished'. It's a short parenthetical noting the movement matched another's will or the force of the sea.
segments.inferno-26-g145: 'infin che' = 'until'. '’l mar fu sovra noi richiuso' is a passive/state description in the past: 'the sea was closed over us' ('fu' = past 'was', 'richiuso' = past participle 'closed'). 'sovra noi' = 'over us' — the sea literally closed in above them.
segments.inferno-26-g54: A vocative address: Maestro mio = “my master.” In Italian the possessive mio follows the noun in such direct-address phrases; the comma marks direct speech address.
segments.inferno-26-g55: rispuos' io is the poet’s contracted form of risposi io (passato remoto, 1st person). The apostrophe shows an elision of the final vowel for metrical reasons; it means “I replied.” Verb before subject is a common narrative inversion.
segments.inferno-26-g56: per udirti = purpose infinitive (literally “in order to hear you”); son io più certo is inverted present of essere with the subject pronoun postposed: “I am more certain.” più modifies certo as a comparative.
segments.inferno-26-g57: ma già m’era avviso che così fosse uses the impersonal expression m'era avviso (literally “there was to me a notice/opinion”), indicating a past suspicion. Fos­se is the imperfect subjunctive of essere in the subordinate clause after a past-tense mental verb (‘I had supposed that...’).
segments.inferno-26-g58: e già voleva dirti: — voleva is the imperfect of volere and expresses past intention (“I was about to/would have told you”); dirti is the infinitive with enclitic object ti (“to tell you”).
segments.inferno-26-g59: chi è ’n quel foco = “who is in that fire.” ’n is a poetic contraction of in. che vien sì diviso di sopra is a relative clause: vien (present indicative) = “comes/appears,” diviso is a past participle used adjectivally (“split/divided”), di sopra = “above.”
segments.inferno-26-g60: che par surger de la pira = “which seems to rise from the pyre.” par is present “seems” and is followed by the (archaic) infinitive surger (sorgere). de = from; pira is the funeral pyre. dov’ Eteòcle col fratel fu miso is a relative clause: dov’ = dove (where/whence); fu miso is an archaic passive perfect (miso = placed, an older form of messo) meaning “was placed/laid.”
segments.inferno-26-g61: A single punctuation mark: period closing the quoted speech or sentence; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-26-g62: Rispuose a me is passato remoto third person (‘he answered me’): rispuose (rispose) + indirect object a me. It introduces Virgil’s reply.
segments.inferno-26-g63: Là dentro = locative “there within.” si martira is a pronominal/passive use of martirare meaning “are tormented” (present); the subject Ulisse e Dïomede follows the verb (inverted order for poetic/narrative effect).
segments.inferno-26-g64: e così insieme a la vendetta vanno come a l’ira uses vanno (present indicative, 3pl) = “they go” with a la vendetta = “to vengeance”; come a l’ira is a simile/comparison “as (to) wrath.” insieme and così qualify the manner (“thus together”).
segments.inferno-26-g65: e dentro da la lor fiamma si geme = “and within their flame one/they groan” — si geme is impersonal/pronominal (“is groaned”/“there is groaning”). l’agguato del caval che fé la porta = “the ambush of the horse that made the gate”: fé is past historic (fece) “made,” caval is poetic for cavallo (the Trojan Horse). onde uscì de’ Romani il gentil seme = “whence the noble seed/offspring of the Romans issued.”
segments.inferno-26-g66: Piangevisi is an impersonal/pronominal form meaning “there is weeping” or “they lament there.” entro l’arte per che = “within the art/by which” — arte here means the cunning/skill (the stratagem of the horse), and per che = “by which/for which.”
segments.inferno-26-g67: morta is a feminine past participle used as an adjectival apposition (“dead”), referring forward to Deïdamìa; the comma sets it off as a parenthetical modifier.
segments.inferno-26-g68: Deïdamìa ancor si duol d’Achille = present-tense statement: Deidamia still (ancor) grieves (si duol) for Achilles. si duol is a pronominal verb meaning “mourns/grieves.”
segments.inferno-26-g69: e del Palladio pena vi si porta = “and the sorrow/penalty of the Palladium is borne there.” del Palladio = “of the Palladium” (the sacred image/thing), pena vi si porta is a pronominal passive/impersonal meaning “sorrow is carried/borne there.”
segments.inferno-26-g31: Quante functions as a comparative 'as many/as much as' introducing a simile; the clause that follows names the comparison. 'l' is the definite article; 'villan' is the noun 'peasant' and 'chollowed by a relative clause. 'al poggio' = 'on the hill' (a + il contracted to al). 'si riposa' is reflexive present (literally 'rests himself' / 'is resting').
segments.inferno-26-g32: 'nel tempo che' = 'at the time when', a temporal conjunction introducing the subordinate clause. 'colui che schiara il mondo' = 'he who enlightens the world' (colui + relative che). 'la faccia sua a noi tien meno ascosa' = literal word order 'keeps his face less hidden to us' — 'tien' is present indicative of tenere and 'ascosa' is a past participle/adjective 'hidden'.
segments.inferno-26-g33: Comparative 'come' = 'as/like'. 'la mosca' (the fly) is the subject and 'cede a la zanzara' is the present tense 'gives way to / yields to the mosquito' (cede + a + object).
segments.inferno-26-g34: 'vede' is present indicative 'sees' with direct object 'lucciole' (fireflies). The adverbial phrase 'giù per la vallea' = 'down through the valley' (giù = down; per + the valley). 'vallea' is an archaic spelling of 'valle'.
segments.inferno-26-g35: Forse = 'perhaps'. 'colà' should mean 'there' (here the provided sense list doesn't include this meaning). 'dov' = 'where'; the construction 'dov' e' vendemmia e ara' is elliptical: e' (clitic for 'egli') + verbs 'vendemmia' and 'ara' are 3rd-person present forms meaning 'he/one harvests and ploughs' — so 'where he harvests and ploughs'.
segments.inferno-26-g36: 'Di tante fiamme' is a partitive/causal phrase 'from/with so many flames'. 'tutta risplendea' inverts the normal order: 'tutta' (completely/all over) + imperfect 'risplendea' = 'shone all over/was shining'. 'l'ottava bolgia' is the subject 'the eighth bolgia' (bolgia = Infernal ditch). The imperfect tense gives an ongoing past action.
segments.inferno-26-g37: 'sì com' io m'accorsi' = 'as I noticed' (sì com' = 'just as'; m'accorsi is past 'I realized/observed'). 'tosto che' is a temporal conjunction 'as soon as', followed by 'fui' (preterite of essere) 'I was' and ''ve' = 'where'. 'l fondo parea' = 'the bottom appeared/seemed'.
segments.inferno-26-g38: Opening simile 'E qual colui che...' = 'And like him who...'. 'si vengi�' is reflexive past 'was avenged' / 'took vengeance' (here 'vengi�' = 'avenged himself'); 'con li orsi' = 'by/with the bears' (biblical allusion). 'vide' is past 'saw' and 'al dipartire' ('at the departing') is al + infinitive expressing simultaneous action with the seeing.
segments.inferno-26-g39: 'quando' introduces a temporal clause. 'i cavalli ... levorsi' = 'the horses rose up' — 'erti' modifies 'cavalli' meaning 'upright/erect'. 'levorsi' is a past reflexive form 'rose up' (literally 'raised themselves').
segments.inferno-26-g40: 'che nol potea sì con li occhi seguire' = result clause 'so that he could not follow it with his eyes'. 'nol' is the clitic negative + object ('not it/him') preceding the imperfect 'potea' (could). Word order places the clitic before the verb.
segments.inferno-26-g41: The construction 'non potea ... che' takes a subjunctive in the subordinate clause: 'ch'el vedesse altro che la fiamma sola' means 'that he should see anything other than the lone flame'. 'vedesse' is the imperfect subjunctive (or past subjunctive) used after the negative restrictive 'non potea che'.
segments.inferno-26-g42: 'sì come nuvoletta' is a short simile: 'just like a little cloud'. 'nuvoletta' is a diminutive (small cloud/puff).
segments.inferno-26-g43: Infinitive phrase 'in sù salire' = 'to rise up' or 'rising up'; it continues the descriptive image of the flame mounting like a cloud. 'in sù' is a prepositional adverbial 'upwards'.
segments.inferno-26-g44: 'tal si move ciascuna' = 'thus each (one) moves itself' — inversion places 'tal' (such/so) first for emphasis. 'per la gola del fosso' = 'along the throat/gorge of the ditch' (gola = mouth/gorge), indicating the path each flame takes.
segments.inferno-26-g45: 'ché' gives the reason 'for' or 'because'. 'nessuna mostra 'l furto' = 'none shows the theft' (i.e. no flame reveals the stolen thing). 'mostra' is present indicative 'shows/reveals'.
segments.inferno-26-g46: 'e ogne fiamma un peccatore invola' = 'and every flame carries off / snatches away a sinner.' 'ogne' = every; 'invola' is present (or historic present) meaning 'snatches away / flies off with'.
segments.inferno-26-g47: 'Io stava' is the first-person imperfect 'I was standing/was' (stare used for a continuous past). 'sovra 'l ponte' = 'on the bridge'. 'a veder surto' uses 'a' + infinitive to express purpose 'to see' and 'surto' (past participle/adjective) means 'risen/sprung up' (what he was watching).
segments.inferno-26-g48: 'sì che' = 'so that/therefore'. 's'io' is the contracted conditional 'se io' = 'if I'. The sequence 'non avessi ... preso' is an (elliptical) past subjunctive/periphrastic conditional ('if I had not taken/grabbed a crag'). Note that 'avessi' here functions as the auxiliary in a past subjunctive construction.
segments.inferno-26-g49: 'caduto sarei' = conditional perfect 'I would have fallen'. 'giù' = 'down'. 'sanz' is the contracted 'senza' = 'without', followed by an infinitive clause 'esser urto' = 'there being a blow/impact' (literally 'to be impact'). The phrase means 'I would have fallen down without being struck' or 'without any collision to stop me'.
segments.inferno-26-g50: 'E 'l duca' = 'And the guide' (duca here = guide, Virgil). 'che mi vide tanto atteso' is a relative clause 'who saw me so intent/attentive' — 'vide' past indicative and 'atteso' is a past participle used adjectivally describing Dante's attentive stance.
segments.inferno-26-g51: Single verb 'disse' = 'he said', passato remoto/past simple. It introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-26-g52: In the reported speech 'Dentro dai fuochi son li spirti' the inversion 'son li spirti' (are the spirits) is idiomatic; 'dentro dai fuochi' = 'inside the fires/within the flames'. The provided sense list for 'spirti' doesn't capture the correct meaning 'spirits/souls', so that entry is irregular.
segments.inferno-26-g53: 'catun si fascia' = 'each one wraps himself' (catun = ciascuno/each one; si fascia = reflexive 'wraps himself'). 'di quel ch'elli è inceso' is a relative clause 'with that with which he is inflamed/has been set on fire' — 'quel' is neuter 'that which' and 'inceso' is a past participle 'set alight/inflamed'.
segments.inferno-26-g116: 'O' is the vocative interjection ('Oh'/'O') calling attention; 'frati' is the addressed plural noun 'brothers' (vocative/nominal). The comma marks direct address.
segments.inferno-26-g117: 'dissi' is the first-person singular passato remoto of dire ('I said'). 'che' introduces a subordinate (relative) clause meaning 'who/that' addressing the addressees. 'per cento milia perigli' uses 'per' = 'through' + 'cento milia' = 'a hundred thousand' + 'perigli' = 'dangers/perils' (expressing means by which they came). 'siete giunti' is a compound past (present perfect / passato prossimo) formed with essere as auxiliary (siete) + past participle giunti = 'you have arrived/reached'. 'a l’ occidente' = 'to the West' (l' = the, elided).
segments.inferno-26-g118: 'a' marks the indirect object/beneficiary ('to/for this ...'). 'questa' = 'this' and modifies 'vigilia'. 'tanto picciola' = 'so very small/tiny' (tanto adverb + adjective). 'vigilia' = 'vigil/short watch' (here: a brief remaining period of perception). 'd’ i nostri sensi' = 'of our senses' (genitive). 'ch’ è del rimanente' is a relative clause ('which is of the remaining'), qualifying the vigilia as that which remains. 'non vogliate negar' uses 'non' negative + 'vogliate' (present subjunctive / polite imperative 2pl of 'vogliare' meaning 'be willing' / 'refuse'); together it functions as a polite negative command: 'do not be unwilling to deny' → here: 'do not refuse'. 'negar' is the infinitive 'to deny/withhold' and 'l’ esperïenza' is the direct object 'the experience'. The diaeresis in esperïenza marks pronunciation (three syllables).
segments.inferno-26-g119: 'di retro' is a fixed locution meaning 'behind' (literally 'of/back'); 'al' = a + il 'to the/at the'; 'sol' here is archaic spelling for 'sole' = 'the Sun' (so the phrase means 'behind the sun' or 'beyond the sun'). Note: 'sol' in the provided senses list does not include 'sun', so that sense is not listed there.
segments.inferno-26-g120: 'del' = 'of the'; 'mondo' = 'world' (earth/humanity); 'sanza' is the archaic form of 'senza' = 'without'; 'gente' = 'people/inhabitants'. Together: 'of the world without people' (i.e. a world devoid of inhabitants).
segments.inferno-26-g121: 'Considerate' is the second-person plural imperative (or polite exhortation) 'Consider' addressed to the group. 'la vostra semenza' = 'your seed/lineage/origin' (vostra = your; semenza = origin/lineage). The colon marks the following explanation.
segments.inferno-26-g122: This clause expresses a general truth in a past/passive sense: 'fatti' is the past participle 'made' (from fare) used predicatively. The structure 'fatti non foste a viver' reads as 'you were not made to live' — 'foste' is the second-person plural passato remoto of essere ('you were'), here combined with the participle 'fatti'. 'a viver' = 'to live' (infinitive); 'come bruti' = 'like brutes'. The word order (participle before the auxiliary) is stylistic/archaic but conveys 'you were made not to live as brutes.'
segments.inferno-26-g123: 'ma' = 'but'. 'per seguir' = 'in order to pursue' or 'for the purpose of following' (per + infinitive expresses purpose). 'virtute e canoscenza' = 'virtue and knowledge' (note archaic spelling 'canoscenza' = 'conoscenza'). The clause states the true purpose: to pursue virtue and knowledge.
segments.inferno-26-g124: 'Li miei compagni' = 'my companions' (li the definite article + plural noun). 'fec’ io' is an archaic contraction of 'feci io' (passato remoto of fare): 'I made/caused.' 'sì aguti' = 'so eager/sharp' — aguti (lit. 'sharpened') used figuratively 'sharpened/urged them on.' The comma marks a pause before the instrumental phrase.
segments.inferno-26-g125: 'con' = 'with'; 'questa' = 'this'; 'orazion' = 'prayer/plea' (archaic spelling); 'picciola' = 'small/brief'. The phrase means 'with this small prayer' and states the means by which he urged them.
segments.inferno-26-g126: 'al cammino' = 'on the path / for the journey' (a + il = al). It sets the scene: the urging was directed toward continuing on the way.
segments.inferno-26-g127: 'che' introduces a result clause ('so that/so...that'). 'a pena' is an idiom meaning 'scarcely' or 'with difficulty'; 'poscia' = 'afterwards/later'. 'li avrei ritenuti' is a conditional perfect: 'I would have held them back' (avrei = conditional auxiliary 'would have', ritenuti = past participle 'restrained/kept back'); the sense is 'I would hardly afterward have been able to restrain them.'
segments.inferno-26-g128: 'e' = 'and'. 'volta' is the past participle (from volgere) used here as 'turned' (our stern was turned). 'nostra poppa' = 'our stern' (poppa = stern of the ship). 'nel mattino' = 'toward/in the morning' — indicating the time/direction when they changed course.
segments.inferno-26-g129: 'de’' = contracted form of 'dei'/'de' (of the). 'remi' = 'oars'. 'facemmo' = passato remoto 'we made' (from fare). 'ali' = 'wings' (metaphorical: oars as wings). 'al folle volo' = 'for the mad flight' (to/for the wild/mad flight): 'al' = a + il, 'folle' = 'mad/rash', 'volo' = 'flight'.
segments.inferno-26-g130: 'sempre' = 'always/continually'. 'acquistando' is the present gerund of 'acquistare' here meaning 'gaining' (making progress, gaining ground). 'dal' = da + il 'from the'. 'lato mancino' = 'left side' (mancino = left). The clause means they steadily gained on the left-hand side.
segments.inferno-26-g131: The clause places the direct object phrase 'Tutte le stelle già de l’altro polo' before the verb 'vedea' and the subject 'la notte' at the end: a common poetic inversion where the thing seen is fronted for emphasis and the agent ('la notte') is posited after the verb. 'Vedea' is the imperfect indicative (older spelling), meaning 'was seeing' or 'saw' in a narrative past; here it personifies 'la notte' as the subject doing the seeing.
segments.inferno-26-g132: This is an elliptical coordinated phrase ('e' = 'and') describing 'our' position as 'so low'. The clitic article 'l' (il) appears before the possessive 'nostro', a poetic/archaic presentation meaning 'our [state/position]' with the substantive omitted and understood from context. 'Tanto basso' is a degree phrase ('so/so very low') modifying that implied noun.
segments.inferno-26-g133: The conjunction 'che' introduces a result/consequence clause ('so... that...') explaining how low 'ours' was. 'Non' negates the verb 'surgëa', an imperfect form of 'sorgere' (here spelled with older orthography) meaning 'rose' or 'appeared'; the verb is indicative, narrating past habitual/state. 'Fuor' is an older form of 'fuori' ('out'), and 'del marin suolo' literally 'of the marine ground' = 'out of the sea's surface/sea-floor', completing the idea that our [position] did not rise above the sea.
segments.inferno-26-g87: Poi che introduces a subordinate temporal clause meaning 'after' or 'since' (literally 'then that / after that'). The clause has the subject la fiamma and the compound past venuta with auxiliary fu (fu venuta = 'had come/arrived'); quivi is an adverb 'there'; dove parve al mio duca introduces a relative/locative clause where parve (passato remoto) means 'seemed/was judged fitting' and al mio duca ('to my guide') is the indirect object; tempo e loco are the things that 'seemed' to him.
segments.inferno-26-g88: in questa forma is an adverbial phrase 'in this form/appearance'; lui is object pronoun 'him' (i.e. the guide) and parlare is an infinitive complement to audivi (audivi + infinitive = 'I heard him speak'); audivi is imperfect 'I heard'.
segments.inferno-26-g89: O voi che siete due dentro ad un foco is a vocative address: O voi ('O you') followed by a relative-like clause che siete...; dentro ad un foco uses dentro + a to indicate 'within one fire' — voi is plural 'you', siete is present 'are'.
segments.inferno-26-g90: s’io meritai is conditional/archaic 'if I merited' (s’ = se). mentre ch’io vissi = 'while I lived'; meritai is a past tense (here a perfect/remote) meaning 'I deserved' or 'I earned'; visti/vissi in Dante can be irregular — here the clause states a hypothetical about past life-time.
segments.inferno-26-g91: Parallel conditional s’io meritai ... assai o poco quando nel mondo li alti versi scrissi: the first clause repeats the 'if I merited' formula; assai o poco = 'much or little'; quando introduces the time 'when/in which' I wrote those lofty verses (scrissi is the past historic 'I wrote'). li alti versi = 'the lofty verses'.
segments.inferno-26-g92: non vi movete is an imperative negative 'do not move' addressed to the plural voi; vi is the reflexive/object pronoun referring to 'yourselves' or 'you there'; movete is 2nd-person plural imperative.
segments.inferno-26-g93: ma l’un di voi dica is a command/entreaty: ma 'but', l’un di voi 'one of you' (l’ = the, uno = one), dica is present subjunctive/imperative of dire used to mean 'let one of you say/tell'.
segments.inferno-26-g94: per lui is a short adverbial phrase 'because of him / on his account' (per = 'for/on account of'); lui is the pronoun 'him'.
segments.inferno-26-g95: perduto a morir gissi is a poetic past description: perduto is past participle used adjectivally 'lost/ruined'; a morir = 'to die'; gissi is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'went' or 'were put' (here 'went to die') — the whole phrase means roughly 'where you were lost and went to die'.
segments.inferno-26-g96: Lo maggior corno de la fiamma antica is a noun phrase 'the larger horn of the ancient flame' (maggior is comparative/adjectival). cominciò a + infinitive (crollarsi) expresses the beginning of an action; crollarsi is reflexive here meaning 'to shake/tremble', and mormorando is a present participle/gerund giving the manner 'murmuring'.
segments.inferno-26-g97: pur come quella cui vento affatica is a simile 'just like that which the wind wearies'; pur come = 'just as / even as'; cui is the relative pronoun 'which/whom'; affatica is present tense 'wearies/tires'.
segments.inferno-26-g98: indi la cima qua e là menando uses indi 'then/from there' (adverb), la cima 'the tip/top' and menando (gerund from menare) 'moving/leading' — qua e là 'here and there' describes the motion of the tip.
segments.inferno-26-g99: come fosse la lingua che parlasse is a hypothetical simile: fosse and parlasse are imperfect subjunctives ('as if it were...that spoke'), a common Dantean device; la lingua is personified as if it were speaking.
segments.inferno-26-g100: gittò voce di fuori e disse uses gittò (perfect) + voce 'uttered a voice' i.e. 'uttered' or 'sent forth a voice'; di fuori 'outward' and disse is simple past 'said'.
segments.inferno-26-g101: Quando mi diparti’ da Circe begins a temporal clause 'When I departed from Circe'; diparti’ is an archaic past form of 'dipartire' (to depart); mi is the object pronoun 'me'.
segments.inferno-26-g102: che sottrasse me più d’un anno là presso a Gaeta is a relative clause where che = 'who' and sottrasse is a past tense meaning 'kept back / detained'; più d’un anno = 'more than a year' and là presso a Gaeta = 'there near Gaeta'.
segments.inferno-26-g103: prima che sì Enëa la nomasse uses prima che + subjunctive (nomasse) 'before Aeneas so named her'; sì here means 'so' or 'thus' and nomasse is imperfect subjunctive/past subjunctive of 'to name/call'.
segments.inferno-26-g104: né dolcezza di figlio is an item in a negative list introduced by né 'nor'; dolcezza di figlio = 'the tenderness/sweetness of a son'.
segments.inferno-26-g105: né la pieta del vecchio padre continues the negative list: la pieta 'the pity/compassion' of the old father; pieta is a noun and del vecchio padre is a possessive genitive phrase.
segments.inferno-26-g106: né ’l debito amore lo qual dovea Penelopè far lieta continues 'nor the dutiful love that should have made Penelope happy'; lo qual is an archaic relative 'which/that' and dovea + infinitive expresses obligation/expected consequence 'ought to/must have'.
segments.inferno-26-g107: vincer potero dentro a me l’ardore ... is a complex clause: vincer (infinitive 'to overcome/prevail') with potero (future 'was able' or 'could') indicates inability of those affections to prevail; dentro a me 'within me' and the relative clause ch’i’ ebbi a divenir explains the ardore (the ardor I had to become experienced). Ebbi is perfect/past 'I had' and divenir is infinitive 'to become'.
segments.inferno-26-g108: ma misi me per l’alto mare aperto sol con un legno e con quella compagna picciola da la qual non fui diserto: ma = 'but'; misi me (past 'I put myself') is a reflexive perfect meaning 'I set out'; per l’alto mare aperto = 'across the open deep sea'; sol (solo) con un legno = 'alone with one planking/boat' (legno here = small craft), and da la qual non fui diserto = 'from which I was not deserted' (non fui diserto = 'I was not abandoned').
segments.inferno-26-g109: L’un lito e l’altro vidi is a coordinated phrase 'one shore and the other I saw' where the article + uno (L’un) emphasizes 'one (shore)'; vidi is simple past 'I saw'; infin la Spagna 'as far as Spain' uses infin = 'as far as'.
segments.inferno-26-g110: fin nel Morrocco = 'even to / up to Morocco'; fin (short for fino) + nel = 'as far as into the' indicates extent/direction.
segments.inferno-26-g111: e l’isola d’i Sardi = 'and the island of the Sardinians' (d’i = di + i, the genitive of the people/inhabitants).
segments.inferno-26-g112: e l’altre che quel mare intorno bagna is 'and the others which that sea bathes around' — che is a relative pronoun 'which', intorno 'around', bagna ('bathes/washes') is present tense third person singular describing the sea's action.
segments.inferno-26-g113: Io e ’ compagni eravam vecchi e tardi quando venimmo a quella foce stretta: subject Io e compagni 'I and my companions' with eravam (imperfect plural) 'we were'; vecchi e tardi = 'old and late'; quando venimmo = 'when we came' to quella foce stretta 'that narrow mouth' (foce = estuary/river-mouth). The clause dov’ Ercule segnò li suoi riguardi uses dov’ (dove) 'where' and segnò (past) 'marked'.
segments.inferno-26-g114: da la man destra mi lasciai Sibilia = 'from the right hand I left Seville' where da la man destra describes the side/direction (from the right), mi lasciai is past 'I left (behind) myself' = 'I left (i.e. departed from) Seville'; Sibilia is the place-name.
segments.inferno-26-g115: da l’altra già m’avea lasciata Setta = 'from the other (side) Setta had already left me'; già m’avea lasciata is pluperfect/compound sense 'had already left me' (avea + past participle), with Setta as the place/name (here the sense list doesn't match the proper place-name).

sentences.inferno-26-s5: So he was, since indeed he must be so!
sentences.inferno-26-s6: for it will weigh on me more, the more I grow older.
sentences.inferno-26-s7: We set out, and up along the stairways that he had earlier made of ivory for going down, my guide climbed up again and pulled me; and, continuing along the solitary way, among the shards and among the crags of the rock my foot, without the hand, could not hasten.
sentences.inferno-26-s8: Then I was pained, and now I grieve again when I turn my mind to what I saw; and I restrain my wit more than I am wont, so that it will not run unless virtue guides it; so that, if a good star or some better thing has given me this good, I may not begrudge it to myself.
sentences.inferno-26-s1: Rejoice, Florence, now that you are so great that you beat your wings over sea and land, and your name spreads throughout Hell!
sentences.inferno-26-s2: Among the thieves I found five of your citizens of that sort, which gives me shame, and you do not rise to great honor on their account.
sentences.inferno-26-s3: But if, near the true morning, one dreams, you will hear, from here after a short time, of what Prato — not others — longs for you.
sentences.inferno-26-s4: And if it were already so, it would not be in time.
sentences.inferno-26-s17: 'If they can speak from within those sparks,' I said, 'Master, I beg and beg you earnestly — and may my entreaty be worth a thousand — that you do not force me to be deprived of waiting until the horned flame comes here; see that I bend toward her with desire!'
sentences.inferno-26-s19: And he to me: "Your request is deserving of much praise, and therefore I accept it; but see that your tongue hold itself back."
sentences.inferno-26-s20: Let me speak, for I have grasped what you want; they would be shy, because they are Greeks, perhaps with regard to your words.
sentences.inferno-26-s29: Five times rekindled and as many times gone out was the light beneath the moon, after we had entered into the high pass, when there appeared to us a mountain, dark because of the distance, and it seemed to me as tall as any I had ever seen.
sentences.inferno-26-s30: We cheered ourselves, and soon it turned to weeping; for from the new land a whirlwind arose and struck the ship's first plank.
sentences.inferno-26-s31: Three times he made it spin with all the waters; on the fourth he raised the stern up and the prow went down, as others wished, until the sea was closed over us.
sentences.inferno-26-s13: “My master,” I replied, “to hear you I am the more certain; but I had already suspected that it would be so, and I was already going to tell you: who is in that fire which comes so split above, which seems to rise from the pyre where Eteocles was placed with his brother?”
sentences.inferno-26-s15: He answered me: “There within Ulysses and Diomedes are tormented, and thus together they go to vengeance as to wrath; and within their flame is groaned the ambush of the horse that made the gate from which the noble seed of the Romans issued.”
sentences.inferno-26-s16: There is weeping within the art by which, though dead, Deidamia still grieves for Achilles, and the sorrow of the Palladium is borne there.”
sentences.inferno-26-s9: As the peasant who rests on the hill, at the time when he who enlightens the world keeps his face less hidden from us, just as the fly yields to the mosquito, sees fireflies down through the valley, perhaps there where he harvests and ploughs: from so many flames the eighth bolgia shone all over, as I perceived as soon as I was where the bottom seemed.
sentences.inferno-26-s10: And like the man who was avenged by the bears saw Elijah's chariot at its departure, when the horses, erect toward the sky, rose up, so that he could not follow it with his eyes, that he should see anything other than the flame alone, rising like a little cloud: thus each moves up the throat of the ditch, for none shows the theft, and every flame carries off a sinner.
sentences.inferno-26-s11: I was standing on the bridge to watch the rising, so that if I had not grabbed a crag, I would have fallen down without any impact.
sentences.inferno-26-s12: And the guide, who saw me so intent, said: 'Within the fires are the spirits; each one wraps himself in that with which he is inflamed.'
sentences.inferno-26-s25: "O brothers," I said, "who, through a hundred thousand perils, have come to the West, do not refuse to this tiny vigil of our remaining senses the experience of going behind the sun, into a world without people."
sentences.inferno-26-s26: Consider your origin: you were not made to live like brutes, but to pursue virtue and knowledge.
sentences.inferno-26-s27: I so spurred my companions, with this little prayer, on the way that afterward I would scarcely have been able to hold them back; and when morning turned our stern, we made wings of the oars for the mad flight, always gaining on the left side.
sentences.inferno-26-s28: Night already saw all the stars of the other pole, and ours so low that it did not rise above the sea's surface.
sentences.inferno-26-s21: After the flame had come there, where it seemed to my guide time and place, I heard him speak in this form: “O you who are two within one fire, if I deserved anything from you while I lived, if I deserved much or little from you when I wrote those lofty verses in the world, do not move; but let one of you say where, on his account, you were lost and went to die.”
sentences.inferno-26-s22: The larger horn of the ancient flame began to rock itself murmuring, just like one that the wind wearies; then, moving its tip here and there, as if it were a tongue that spoke, it cast a voice outward and said: "When I departed from Circe, who kept me more than a year near Gaeta, before Aeneas so named her, neither a son's sweetness, nor the pity of the old father, nor the dutiful love that should have made Penelope happy were able to overcome within me the ardor I had to become experienced in the world and in human vices and in valor; but I put myself upon the open high sea alone with one plank and with that small company from which I was not deserted."
sentences.inferno-26-s23: I saw one shore and the other as far as Spain, even to Morocco, and the island of the Sardinians, and the others that that sea bathes around.
sentences.inferno-26-s24: My companions and I were old and late when we came to that narrow mouth where Hercules marked his bounds so that man should not go farther; from the right hand I left Seville, from the other Setta had already left me.
